subtopic: Forest Conservation Act and Green India Mission
title: Forest Clearance and Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act, 2023
focus: Statutory procedures for forest clearance, redefined scope of 'forest', and exemptions under the 2023 Amendment Act, including the Godavarman judgment context.
rationale: The 2023 Amendment is highly testable for UPSC Prelims due to controversies over the definition of forests and strategic exemptions.
sectionExcerpt: Procedure for Forest Clearance involves Stage I (In-Principle) and Stage II (Final). Approval Authority varies: up to 5 ha by Chief Conservator, above 5 ha or mining by MoEFCC. The Forest (Conservation) Amendment Act, 2023 defines forest as land notified under laws like Indian Forest Act 1927 or recorded in govt records on/after Oct 25, 1980. It exempts land changed to non-forest use before 1996. Supreme Court directed following the 1996 Godavarman dictionary meaning until identification is complete. Exemptions include land within 100 km of borders for national security, up to 10 ha for security infrastructure, and up to 5 ha in LWE areas.
evidenceBullets: Stage I clearance requires conditions like funding compensatory afforestation; Stage II is final clearance.
MoEFCC grants clearance for projects above 5 hectares or any mining proposals.
2023 Act exempts forest land within 100 kms of international borders or LAC for national security projects.
Exempts up to 5 hectares for defence or public utility projects in Left-Wing Extremism-affected areas.
Godavarman case (1996) expanded forest definition to include dictionary meaning and all government records irrespective of ownership.

subtopic: Forest Conservation Act and Green India Mission
title: National Mission for a Green India (GIM)
focus: Objectives, funding, implementation mechanisms, and key strategies of the Green India Mission under NAPCC.
rationale: Core climate change initiative linked to India's NDCs, carbon sink targets, and CAMPA funding, making it a standard environment topic.
sectionExcerpt: Launched in 2014 under NAPCC. Objectives include increasing forest/tree cover over 24 million hectares and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3.0 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent by 2030. Funding comes from Mission allocation and National CAMPA Fund. Implemented via a bottom-up model with Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMCs). Strategies include Micro-Ecosystem Approach for vulnerable zones, leveraging CSR, selling carbon credits via voluntary markets, and deploying a Green India Force of trained youth.
evidenceBullets: GIM is one of the 8 missions under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC).
Aims to create an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3.0 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent by 2030.
Funding is partially sourced from the National CAMPA Fund.
Implementation relies on a bottom-up model with Joint Forest Management Committees (JFMCs).
Strategies include selling carbon credits from forestry and agroforestry via voluntary markets.

subtopic: Forest Conservation Act and Green India Mission
title: Environmental Accounting on Forest 2025 and ISFR 2023
focus: MoSPI's forest accounting report based on the UN SEEA framework and the latest forest cover statistics from ISFR 2023.
rationale: ISFR data is a staple for UPSC Prelims geography and environment questions, and the new SEEA-based accounting report introduces a novel economic-environmental metric.
sectionExcerpt: Environmental Accounting on Forest 2025 Report released by MoSPI is the first dedicated publication on forest accounting based on the UN System of Environmental Economic Accounts (SEEA) Framework. SEEA integrates economic and environmental data. ISFR 2023 shows total forest and tree cover increased to 25.17 percent of geographical area (21.76 percent Forest cover, 3.41 percent Tree cover). Maximum increase seen in Chhattisgarh, UP, Odisha, and Rajasthan. 8 states/UTs have forest cover above 75 percent, including Mizoram, Lakshadweep, A&N Islands, and Arunachal Pradesh.
evidenceBullets: Environmental Accounting on Forest 2025 Report was released by the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI).
The report is based on the UN System of Environmental Economic Accounts (SEEA) Framework developed in 2012.
ISFR 2023 reports total forest and tree cover at 25.17 percent of India's geographical area.
Maximum increase in forest cover was recorded in Chhattisgarh, Uttar Pradesh, Odisha, and Rajasthan.
8 states/UTs, including Mizoram and Lakshadweep, have forest cover above 75 percent of their area.
